description: Kartikeya (Sanskrit: Kartikeya, 'Son of the Krittikas') is the Hindu god of war, victory, and the commander-in-chief of the divine armies (Deva Sena). Born from the fiery seed of Shiva and nursed by the six Krittikas (Pleiades stars), he is also known as Skanda, Murugan, Subrahmanya, and Kumara. With six heads and twelve arms, he wields the divine spear Vel (or Shakti) and rides the magnificent peacock Paravani. Kartikeya was specifically created by the gods to defeat the invincible demon Tarakasura, whom only a son of Shiva could slay. His victory over Taraka and his demon armies established him as the protector of dharma and the embodiment of youthful martial prowess. In South India, particularly Tamil Nadu, he is worshipped as Murugan, the supreme deity with a rich mythological corpus including the Skanda Purana and Tirumurukkatrupadai. He represents the focused, disciplined spiritual warrior who conquers inner demons of ignorance and ego. His peacock mount symbolizes the conquest of pride and sensory attachments, while his spear represents the penetrating wisdom that destroys spiritual obstacles. Along with his brother Ganesha, Kartikeya is one of the beloved sons of Shiva and Parvati in the Hindu pantheon.
title: Kartikeya - Hindu Mythology

sources.title: Skanda Purana
sources.description: Major Purana dedicated to Kartikeya's mythology

sources.title: Hindu Goddesses and Gods

worshipPractices.name: Puja
worshipPractices.description: Puja is a common form of worship involving offering flowers, fruits, incense, and prayers to deities like Kartikeya. Devotees perform pujas at home or in temples, often accompanied by chanting mantras and singing devotional songs.

worshipPractices.name: Fasting
worshipPractices.description: Some devotees observe fasts on specific days associated with Kartikeya to seek his blessings and fulfill vows. These fasts may involve abstaining from certain foods or drinks, and spending the day in prayer and meditation.

festivals.name: Skanda Shasti
festivals.timing: Sixth day of Shukla Paksha in the month of Karthika (October-November)
festivals.description: Dedicated to Kartikeya, this festival commemorates his victory over the demon Surapadman. Devotees observe fasts, perform special pujas, and participate in processions and re-enactments of the battle.

festivals.name: Thaipusam
festivals.timing: Full moon day in the Tamil month of Thai (January-February)
festivals.description: A major festival celebrated predominantly by Tamil Hindus, Thaipusam honors Kartikeya (Murugan). Devotees undertake pilgrimages, carry kavadis (decorated structures), and pierce their bodies with skewers as acts of devotion and penance.

cultural.modernLegacy: Kartikeya's imagery and stories continue to inspire art, literature, and popular culture, particularly in South India. He appears in various forms of visual art, including sculptures, paintings, and murals, and is a popular subject for devotional songs and films. His stories of valor and wisdom are also retold in contemporary literature and children's books.

associations.name: Shiva
associations.significance: As the destroyer and transformer, Shiva represents the cyclical nature of creation, preservation, and destruction inherent in Hindu cosmology.

associations.name: Vishnu
associations.significance: Vishnu, the preserver, embodies the balance and order of the universe, often intervening in various avatars to restore dharma (righteousness).

associations.name: Lakshmi
associations.significance: Lakshmi, the goddess of wealth and prosperity, symbolizes auspiciousness, abundance, and the divine feminine energy that nurtures life.

associations.name: Karma
associations.significance: Karma, the law of cause and effect, underscores the interconnectedness of actions and their consequences, shaping the cycle of rebirth.

associations.name: Dharma
associations.significance: Dharma represents righteousness, moral duty, and the cosmic order that sustains the universe and guides individuals towards ethical conduct.
The number 108 is considered sacred and significant in Hindu traditions, representing the wholeness of existence and often used in mantras and rituals.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents purification, transformation, and the divine energy (Agni) that carries offerings to the gods and symbolizes spiritual enlightenment.

associations.name: Cow
associations.significance: The cow is revered as a sacred animal symbolizing abundance, non-violence (ahimsa), and the nurturing aspect of the Earth.

companions.name: Devasena
companions.description: Commander-in-chief of the divine armies, Kartikeya leads Devasena, the divine army, into battle against the demons.

companions.name: Vel
companions.description: Kartikeya's divine spear, Vel, is a symbol of his power and victory over evil, granted to him by his mother, Parvati.

companions.name: Peacock
companions.description: Kartikeya's vahana (mount) is a peacock, symbolizing his victory over worldly desires and ego.

companions.name: Krittikas
companions.description: The six Krittika sisters nursed and raised Kartikeya after his birth, hence the name 'Son of the Krittikas'.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth of the Universe in Hindu Mythology
extendedContent.content:
Hindu cosmology presents a cyclical view of the universe, with creation, preservation, and destruction occurring in endless cycles known as Kalpas. The origins of the cosmos are often attributed to Brahman, the ultimate reality, which manifests as various deities to initiate the process. One prominent narrative involves the cosmic egg, Hiranyagarbha, from which Brahma, the creator god, emerges to begin the work of creation. This emergence is not a singular event but part of a recurring cycle, emphasizing the transient nature of all existence. The Puranas, such as the Vishnu Purana and Bhagavata Purana, elaborate on this process, describing the creation of elements, celestial beings, and living organisms. 

Another significant origin story revolves around the Samudra Manthan, the churning of the cosmic ocean. Devas (gods) and Asuras (demons) cooperate to churn the ocean using Mount Mandara as a churning rod and Vasuki, the serpent king, as the rope. This churning yields various treasures, including Lakshmi, the goddess of prosperity, and Amrita, the elixir of immortality. However, it also produces Halahala, a deadly poison which Shiva consumes to save the universe, turning his throat blue (hence his name Neelakantha). The Samudra Manthan symbolizes the struggle between good and evil, and the necessity of balance in the cosmos. 

The concept of Maya, cosmic illusion, is also crucial to understanding creation. The perceived reality is considered an illusion projected by Brahman, which veils the true nature of reality. This illusion allows for the experience of duality, separation, and individual existence, which are necessary for the unfolding of cosmic drama. Ultimately, the goal of spiritual practice in Hinduism is to pierce through Maya and realize the oneness of Atman (individual soul) with Brahman, escaping the cycle of birth and death.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples in Hindu Mythology
extendedContent.content:
Worship in Hinduism, known as Puja, is a central practice that involves offering devotion to deities through various rituals and ceremonies. The core of Puja typically involves offering flowers, incense, water, food (Prasada), and chanting mantras. The specific forms of worship vary depending on the deity and the regional traditions. Temples serve as sacred spaces for devotees to connect with the divine, housing the deity's image (Murti) and acting as centers for religious and cultural activities. The construction of temples is often guided by ancient texts like the Shilpa Shastras, which outline precise rules for architecture, iconography, and ritual practices.

Different deities receive varying forms of worship. For example, Shiva is often worshipped through the Lingam, a symbolic representation of his creative energy, while Vishnu is worshipped in his various avatars, such as Rama and Krishna. Goddesses (Devis) are revered in their diverse forms, including Durga, Lakshmi, and Saraswati, each representing different aspects of divine feminine power. The worship of deities is not merely a symbolic act but is believed to invoke the deity's presence and blessings. Mantras, sacred sounds, play a crucial role in invoking the deities and creating a connection between the devotee and the divine.

Festivals are an integral part of Hindu worship, celebrating events in the lives of deities and commemorating significant mythological events. Diwali, the festival of lights, celebrates Rama's return to Ayodhya after defeating Ravana. Holi, the festival of colors, commemorates the victory of good over evil and the love between Krishna and Radha. These festivals provide opportunities for communal worship, feasting, and the reenactment of mythological stories, reinforcing cultural values and beliefs. The temple is not just a place of worship; it is a community hub where people come together to celebrate their shared faith and heritage.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation of Deities
extendedContent.content:
Hindu deities are not merely abstract concepts but are represented through intricate iconography that conveys specific attributes, powers, and narratives. Each deity is associated with distinct symbols, colors, vehicles (Vahanas), and Mudras (hand gestures) that carry symbolic meaning. For example, Vishnu is often depicted with four arms holding a conch shell (Shankha), a discus (Chakra), a mace (Gada), and a lotus flower (Padma), each representing different aspects of his cosmic power and virtues. Shiva is often depicted with a trident (Trishula), a crescent moon on his head, and a snake around his neck, symbolizing his control over destruction, time, and the cycles of life.

The choice of Vahana, or vehicle, is also significant. Vishnu rides Garuda, the eagle-like bird, symbolizing strength and swiftness. Shiva rides Nandi, the bull, representing strength, virility, and righteousness. Durga rides a lion or a tiger, symbolizing her power and ferocity in defeating evil. The colors associated with deities also hold symbolic meaning. Blue is often associated with Vishnu and Krishna, representing infinity and the cosmic ocean. Red is associated with goddesses like Durga, symbolizing energy and power.

The Murti, or deity image, is not simply a statue but a consecrated object that embodies the divine presence. The creation of a Murti is a sacred process guided by specific guidelines outlined in the Shilpa Shastras. The image is imbued with divine energy through rituals and mantras, transforming it into a conduit for connecting with the deity. Devotees interact with the Murti through Puja, offering prayers, and seeking blessings. The iconography of Hindu deities is a rich and complex language that communicates profound theological and philosophical concepts, allowing devotees to connect with the divine in a tangible and meaningful way.

extendedContent.title: The Role of Mythology in Maintaining Cosmic Order (Dharma)
extendedContent.content:
Hindu mythology plays a vital role in upholding Dharma, the cosmic order and righteousness. Myths often depict the consequences of Adharma (unrighteousness) and the importance of adhering to moral and ethical principles. Stories of deities battling demons and restoring balance to the universe serve as allegories for the ongoing struggle between good and evil within individuals and society. The concept of Karma, the law of cause and effect, is deeply embedded in Hindu mythology, emphasizing that actions have consequences and that individuals are responsible for their choices. Myths illustrate how virtuous actions lead to positive outcomes, while harmful actions lead to suffering.

Avatars, or incarnations, of deities like Vishnu, are central to maintaining Dharma. When Adharma threatens to overwhelm the universe, Vishnu descends to earth in various forms to restore balance and uphold righteousness. The stories of Rama in the Ramayana and Krishna in the Mahabharata are prime examples of avatars who exemplify Dharma and combat evil. These narratives provide moral guidance and inspire individuals to live virtuous lives. The concept of Moksha, liberation from the cycle of birth and death, is also intertwined with Dharma. By following Dharma and performing righteous actions, individuals can purify their Karma and ultimately achieve Moksha.

Myths provide frameworks for understanding the complexities of life and offer solutions to moral dilemmas. They teach the importance of compassion, truthfulness, non-violence (Ahimsa), and selfless service. The epics like the Ramayana and Mahabharata serve as guides for navigating personal and social relationships, highlighting the importance of duty, loyalty, and justice. The stories of mythological figures provide role models for individuals to emulate, inspiring them to live in accordance with Dharma and contribute to the well-being of society. Hindu mythology is not merely a collection of ancient tales but a living tradition that continues to shape moral values and guide individuals towards a righteous and fulfilling life.

shortDescription: Kartikeya is the Hindu god of war, victory, and commander of the divine armies. Born of Shiva's essence, he embodies courage and strategic prowess.
subtitle: The Divine General: Courage, Victory, and Righteous Warfare
